A contentious debate led to a bipartisan compromise that replaced the arbitrarily set interest rates with ones based on interest rates in the economy. title: Does Slower Growth Imply Lower Interest Rates? - Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
description: Does Slower Growth Imply Lower Interest Rates? Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
summary: Moreover, a decline in the natural rate of interest would tend to lower every other real and nominal interest rate inthe economy. Therefore, understanding the linkage between economic growth and the natural rate is crucial forforecasting all types of interest rates. Although forecasts of potential growth orthe natural rate are rarely reported, we can construct reasonable proxies from long-run forecasts of GDP growth, theshort-term interest rate, and inflation. Because we do not directly observe the natural rate of interest or potential trend growth, we construct proxies fromlong-run forecasts of real GDP growth and short-term real interest rates. For instance, Goldman Sachs (2014) recently examined the effect of real per-capita GDP growth onshort-term real interest rates in 20 countries since the early 1800s.

summary: So what is the relationship between demographics and bond yields, and why did it break down? When the ratio of people of working age to those of non-working age (kids, retirees) is high, inflation is low and bond yields fall. Wage growth has historically been a driver of inflationary pressure, so weak wage growth is correlated with low consumer price inflation, and hence low bond yields. Government debt burdens grew, and interest rates and bond yields hit double digits. And, in fact, they have overpowered demographics to leave bond yields in a different paradigm than any model might have predicted.

summary: The yield curve is the difference between long-term interest rates and short-term interest rates, often quantified in the United States as the difference between 10-year Treasury interest rates and 2-year Treasury interest rates. If you were to chart it as a yield curve, it is visually steep like the yield curve chart above. Such a situation looks like this:This is known as an inverted yield curve, or a yield curve inversion, and it’s usually bad news. The Correlation is ImperfectCountries can have recessions without yield curve inversions, and can have yield curve inversions without recessions. Usually, at the very least, an inverted yield curve is associated with an economic slowdown (if not an outright recession), and a recession is at least associated with a flattening of the yield curve (if not an outright yield curve inversion).

Under the so-called expectations hypothesis, long-term interest rates reflect investors’ expectations of future short-term interest rates. Intuitively, no investor would invest in long-term bonds if short-term rates were higher—and were expected to remain indefinitely higher—than long-term rates. summary: PTFs’ share of trading volume-more than half in the futures and interdealer cash markets on October 15 and control days-increased significantly during the event window. Moreover, trading activity of PTFs and bank-dealers in the cash and futures markets is highly concentrated. Only modest changes in net positions occurred during the event window, but there were more buyer-initiated trades as prices rose, and more seller-initiated trades as prices fell. The time required to process incoming futures orders increased just ahead of the event window, with significantly increased message traffic due to order cancellations. “Self-trading” (where the same entity takes both sides of a trade) increased during the event window.

summary: By Komson Silapachai, Research & Portfolio StrategyInvestors have been holding their collective breath for the inevitable increase in 10-year Treasury yields, as many associate a rising interest rate environment with negative fixed income returns. Understandably so, but we recommend zooming out and considering fixed income returns over a longer time horizon. During the last 11 periods of rising interest rates, fixed income investors may have experienced negative total returns while yields were rising, but ultimately, they experienced positive returns over the following 6 and 12 months. Over the rolling two-year period going back to December 1998, rarely have there been periods where fixed income has experienced negative returns. To be a successful fixed income investor, taking a comprehensive approach is key, as near-term total return rarely tells the whole story.

summary: They used to call Bill Gross the Bond King. Gross self-published the book because he felt he had more editorial control and could get it out quicker than with a traditional publishing firm. They estimate the US natural policy rate, at which monetary policy is neither restrictive nor accommodative, at about 3-3.25%, to date (Nov 27, 2023). title: The TIPS bond market sees 2.1% inflation over the next 10 years - The Real Economy Blog
description: The TIPS bond market sees 2.1% inflation over the next 10 years The Real Economy Blog
summary: For households and commercial investors, the TIPS market provides a guaranteed return no matter the effect of inflation. In terms of the investment-decision process, the implied breakeven inflation rate of TIPS yields has been a reliable indicator of inflation expectations, with the TIPS yield providing a market-driven estimate of real interest rates, which are adjusted for inflation. The 10-year TIPS market is anticipating a drop in inflation to reasonable levels, to an average of just under 2.1% over the life of the 10-year bond. For businesses with a five-year investment horizon, the TIPS yield is 1.8% with an anticipated inflation rate of just under 2%. The 3.9% Treasury bond yield can be decomposed into the 1.8% real yield in the TIPS market plus the anticipated inflation rate of 2.1%.

title: European insurers poised to benefit from rising government bond yields: Fitch - reinsurancene.ws
description: European insurers poised to benefit from rising government bond yields: Fitch reinsurancene.ws
summary: Fitch Ratings, a credit rating agency, indicated on March 14, 2025, that the increase in government bond yields, driven by elevated defence spending, is likely to positively impact most European insurers. According to Fitch Ratings, life insurers with a focus on annuities or long-term savings products that include investment guarantees are expected to gain the most from higher bond yields. While the higher bond yields could lead to increased refinancing costs for many European insurers, Fitch does not foresee a major negative impact on the profitability of the insurers it rates. Fitch Ratings also pointed out that the rise in bond yields, alongside greater yield volatility, will directly affect insurers’ Solvency II positions. title: Will the “Big Beautiful Bill” Burn the Bond Market? - Foreign Policy in Focus
description: Will the “Big Beautiful Bill” Burn the Bond Market? Foreign Policy in Focus
summary: In this climate, the passage of Trump’s “Big Beautiful Bill” would be akin to pouring gasoline on a fiscal fire. The United States could face a scenario where rising interest rates and falling bond demand collide violently, triggering a mass sell-off of U.S. securities. Unless a serious course correction is initiated—fiscally, diplomatically, and monetarily—the United States could be hurtling toward not just an economic downturn but a credibility collapse. The Big Beautiful Bill may soon be remembered not as a legislative triumph, but as a monument to America’s fiscal myopia. At this point, the question may no longer be whether the United States hits the wall but how fast and how hard.

title: Market Minute: Falling bond yields and economic imbalances - The Real Economy Blog
description: Market Minute: Falling bond yields and economic imbalances The Real Economy Blog
summary: Long-term interest rates have been falling since the summer, most likely as investors came to terms with the risk of a slowing economy, domestic economic imbalances and the knock-on effects of persistent inflation. The bond market is pricing in something that in past cycles would have looked like a large midcycle slowdown or modest economic correction. Get Joe Brusuelas’s Market Minute economic commentary every morning. In the current cycle, the relationship between 10-year and 30-year Treasury yields has been altered, first with a bear-market selloff of 30-year bonds as the risk of fiscal malfeasance escalated. During this same period, 30-year Treasury yields dropped by only 38 basis points, from roughly 5.0% to 4.6%.

title: Mortgage Rates Declined Despite Higher Treasury Yields - Eye On Housing
description: Mortgage Rates Declined Despite Higher Treasury Yields Eye On Housing
summary: Long-term mortgage rates continued to decline in January. According to Freddie Mac, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage averaged 6.10% last month, 9 basis points (bps) lower than December. While mortgage rates typically move in tandem with the treasury yields, the spread between the two narrowed during the month. Reports that the Trump administration encouraged Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to expand purchases of mortgage-backed securities (MBS) boosted demand for MBS, pushing mortgage rates lower. The 10-year Treasury rate at the beginning of 2026 was at 4.11%.

description: Treasury Yields Spike, 10-Year to 4.39%, 30-Year to 4.96%, Mortgage Rates to 6.5%, as the Bond Market Gets Antsy Wolf Street
summary: Bond market frets about inflation, leveraged Treasury bets unwind, housing market frets about spring selling season. The 10-year Treasury yield spiked by 14 basis points to 4.39% today, the highest since July 2025. Travails in the bond market hit the mortgage market. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate tracks the 10-year Treasury yield, but is higher, and the spread between them varies. The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate spiked to 6.53% today, according to Mortgage News Daily.
